sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Kayla Ward, a 14-year-old female, presents with a 2-year history of acne on her face, which is present most every day.
She reports using Persa-Gel and washing regularly, which is somewhat helpful.
There are no associated symptoms including itching, bleeding, or pain.
She has no additional past medical history.
She lives with her parents and sister, and has a dog, bird, and bunnies.
She is in 7th grade and plays basketball and volleyball and tap.
She wears sunscreen in the summer, SPF 30.
There is no additional family history.
Review of systems: There are no additional symptoms in other organs that are relevant to the initial presentation.
Past medical history: No past medical history is mentioned.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The examination findings from the physical exam include multiple benign nevi on the trunk and extremities, scattered skin-colored papules, open and closed comedones, and erythematous papules on the face, primarily on the forehead and with some on the central cheeks and chin.
The chest and back are relatively spared, and the remainder of the examination is normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has mild to moderate acne, mostly comodonal with a small inflammatory component.
Problem list: Acne; active.
Plan:
I prescribed adapalene 0.1% cream to be applied in a thin layer to the areas she generally gets acne, starting off using it a few nights a week and slowly working up to using it every night.
I also recommended using a mild cleanser and a non-comedogenic moisturizer with SPF 30 or higher every morning, and a non-comedogenic moisturizer at night.
I advised her to use sunscreen when outside due to potential sensitivity from the adapalene.
I also recommended using Clean and Clear Persa-Gel in a thin layer on the areas she generally gets acne.
I will see her for follow-up in six months.
Follow-up: I will see Kayla in six months to reassess her acne.
